writing_task: I'm working on a mid-year update letter for a small group of major donors — people who gave $10,000 or more last year to our workforce reentry program for formerly incarcerated women. These aren't first-time donors, so I don't need to explain the whole program from scratch, but I do need to re-engage them before our fall campaign kicks off. Can you draft 3 short paragraphs: open with a specific woman's story from this past cycle, then transition to what her outcome represents at scale, and close with a forward-looking ask tied directly back to her situation? The tone should feel like a personal update from someone they already trust, not a formal report.

drafts.rubric:
Denise was 41 when she walked into our certification cohort last January, three weeks out of Rikers, with her daughter's school enrollment forms still folded in her coat pocket. She had decided she wasn't going to wait for things to settle before she started moving. By June, she had her pharmacy technician license and a full-time offer at a clinic in the Bronx — the first salaried position she'd held in eleven years.

What Denise did, 34 other women did alongside her this cycle. Of those, 29 are employed within their field of certification, most within 60 days of completing the program. You read that potential correctly when you decided to back this work last year. The numbers are proving the thesis.

Here's where things stand heading into fall: 47 women have already put their names on the waitlist for the January cohort — more than we've ever carried into a new cycle. At $1,200 per participant for the full training sequence, each decision to stay in at your level clears a path for roughly eight of them. Denise keeps her daughter's school forms on the fridge now, all filled out, because she finally has a permanent address. There are 47 more women doing the same kind of math, waiting to see what's possible. If you're in for another round, now's the right moment to say so.
drafts.generic:
Six months ago, Denise walked out of Ricci Correctional carrying a garbage bag and a bus pass. Today she's a certified medical billing specialist, two months into a job that covers her rent and her daughter's after-school program. She still texts our case manager on Fridays — not because she needs anything, but because she wants to report in.

Denise is one of 34 women we've served this cycle. Twenty-six are employed. Eleven have reunified with children. That's not a coincidence — it's what consistent, wrap-around support actually produces when it's properly funded.

We're heading into fall with 40 women on our waitlist, including three who share Denise's exact profile: single mothers, tech-transferable skills, and zero margin for error. Your support last year made Denise possible. We'd love to talk about what this fall could look like with you alongside us again.
drafts.preference:
Earlier this spring, a woman named Daria finished her first full quarter as a medical billing coordinator — a job she landed six weeks after completing our program. What stayed with me wasn't the job offer itself, but something smaller: she told her case manager she finally stopped flinching when someone asked for her work history. That shift — from shame to steadiness — is quiet, but it's the thing that makes everything else possible.

Daria isn't an outlier. This cycle, 73% of program completers secured employment within 90 days, and early data suggests retention at the one-year mark is trending higher than any previous cohort. That durability matters because employment alone doesn't break cycles — sustained employment does. Your investment last year helped us build the wraparound support that gets women past those first fragile months, when most reentry efforts fall apart.

We're heading into our fall campaign with the goal of serving 40 additional women before year's end, and we're roughly $85,000 short of what that requires. If you're open to it, I'd love to talk about whether a gift at or near last year's level makes sense for you right now. Even a conversation would help us plan. I can be reached directly at the number below — and I mean that, not as a formality.

user_reason: Draft A is the strongest by a significant margin. Denise has full agency throughout — she decided, she moved, she earned her license. The school forms detail is specific and earned, and it comes back beautifully in the close, making the ask feel organic rather than tacked on. The donor framing is exactly right: 'You read that potential correctly' positions them as smart investors, not charity givers. The ask is concrete — $1,200 per participant, eight women per gift — tied directly back to Denise's story. Draft B has a genuinely good moment with Daria stopping flinching at her work history, and the 'shame to steadiness' line is lovely. But it slides into 'We' as subject in the second paragraph ('Your investment helped us build'), and the ask goes soft and vague at the end — 'I'd love to talk about whether a gift makes sense' loses the specificity I need. Draft C is the weakest. It opens by describing Denise as carrying a garbage bag, which edges toward dehumanizing framing, and then falls into exactly the pattern I want to avoid: 'we've served,' 'our case manager,' 'what consistent wrap-around support produces.' The non-profit keeps inserting itself as the active party. The close — 'Your support made Denise possible' — is the kind of benefactor framing that makes donors feel virtuous rather than strategic. It also lacks any specific dollar figure or outcome tied to the ask.

criteria_analysis.name: Community and Beneficiary Agency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft consistently makes Denise and the women the grammatical subjects of action — 'She had decided,' '34 other women did alongside her,' '47 women have already put their names on the waitlist' — and frames expansion as a response to community demand. The generic draft uses 'we've served' and 'we'd love to talk,' centering the organization more. The original preference draft similarly centers the organization ('we built,' 'our program,' 'we're heading into') and frames Daria's story more passively ('she landed' is good, but the org is frequently the subject).

criteria_analysis.name: Donor as Strategic Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails this: 'You read that potential correctly when you decided to back this work' and 'each decision to stay in at your level' — framing the donor's contribution as a judgment call validated by results, with zero gratitude language. The generic draft says 'Your support last year made Denise possible,' which is closer to gratitude framing and doesn't credit judgment or foresight. The original preference draft uses 'Your investment' which is better than 'support' but still doesn't explicitly credit strategic thinking, and the closing is more transactional than strategic.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Continuity Through the Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft carries Denise by name into the closing, calls back the school enrollment forms motif ('keeps her daughter's school forms on the fridge now'), and frames the ask as what happens next for 47 women doing 'the same kind of math' — the ask feels like the next chapter. The generic draft mentions Denise's profile type but doesn't bring her back by name in the ask or echo a specific image. The original preference draft doesn't return to Daria by name in the ask paragraph at all; the closing is structurally separate from the story, though the tone stays consistent.

criteria_analysis.name: Story-Led Opening
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Denise's name, age (41), specific location (Rikers), timeline (three weeks out), and a vivid concrete detail (daughter's school enrollment forms folded in her coat pocket) — all before any statistics appear. The generic draft opens with Denise, includes details (garbage bag, bus pass, Friday texts), but the opening detail about 'Ricci Correctional' feels slightly less grounded in specificity than the rubric-guided version, and fewer biographical details are layered. The original preference draft opens well with Daria but leads with her job title rather than a biographical anchor, and the 'stopped flinching' detail, while powerful, is reported speech rather than observed action.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete, Specific Asks
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft includes $1,200 per participant tied directly to outcome (clears a path for roughly eight women), 47 women on the waitlist creating natural urgency, and the scale feels holdable and doable. The generic draft gives 40 women on the waitlist but no dollar figure, no cost-per-unit breakdown, and a vague 'we'd love to talk' close. The original preference draft includes $85,000 gap and 40 women, which is specific, but the dollar figure is a large lump sum rather than a broken-down per-person cost — less holdable, though still concrete.

criteria_analysis.name: Warm but Not Sentimental Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft achieves emotional impact through understatement — 'school forms on the fridge now, all filled out, because she finally has a permanent address' — without a single emotive adjective. The generic draft is warm and conversational ('she still texts our case manager on Fridays') but 'That's not a coincidence — it's what consistent, wrap-around support actually produces when it's properly funded' edges toward institutional advocacy tone. The original preference draft has lovely understatement ('stopped flinching') but 'quiet, but it's the thing that makes everything else possible' tells the reader what to feel slightly more than showing.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion — it centers community agency, frames the donor as a strategic decision-maker with zero gratitude language, carries the narrative motif (school forms) seamlessly through the ask, and delivers a precise cost-per-participant number. The original preference draft is strong on tone and storytelling but doesn't return to its named character in the closing and uses a lump-sum ask rather than a per-person breakdown. The generic draft is competently written but centers the organization, uses gratitude-adjacent framing, and lacks a specific dollar figure in the ask — all non-negotiables for this user.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts communicate their message with precision and zero ambiguity. Each presents data, narrative, and the ask in immediately understandable terms.

Draft A weaves Denise's story through all three paragraphs as a structural throughline, connecting personal narrative to data to the ask with exceptional cohesion. Draft B is coherent but the transition from outcomes data to the waitlist/ask feels slightly more abrupt.
Both drafts are mechanically flawless with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues.
Both follow a clear three-paragraph arc: open with a personal story, present program results, close with the forward-looking ask. Each paragraph has a distinct purpose and appropriate length.

Draft A's closing image of school forms on the fridge is deeply affecting; Draft B's detail about Friday texts is equally humanizing. Both use concrete, sensory details that make the reader care. This is a genuine tie at the highest level.

Both strike the ideal donor-letter tone: confident without being pushy, emotionally resonant without being manipulative, respectful of the reader's intelligence. Draft A is slightly warmer and more conversational; Draft B is slightly crisper and more direct — both are excellent choices.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Both are exceptional fundraising letters, but Draft A edges ahead through its superior coherence — Denise's story threads seamlessly from opening to close (the school forms on the fridge echoing the forms in her pocket), the specific math of donor impact ($1,200 = ~8 women) is more actionable, and the closing is more emotionally and rhetorically complete.

writing_task: I need to put together a short year-end giving appeal — just 3 paragraphs — targeted at lapsed donors who gave to our housing stability program two or three years ago but haven't given since. I want to open with a specific story about a participant, then reconnect them to why they gave in the first place, and close with a clear ask. The program has expanded since they last engaged, so there's a real "here's what's happened and here's where you can plug back in" angle I want to hit. Can you draft something for me?

drafts.rubric:
When Marisol's landlord filed the eviction notice last January, she didn't wait to see what would happen. She reached out to our housing counseling line, showed up to every appointment, negotiated directly with her landlord, and worked out a repayment plan on her own terms. Three months later, she signed a two-year lease extension. That outcome belonged to her — she decided to fight for it.

You saw something like this coming when you made your decision two or three years ago. You read the situation correctly: that people in housing crises weren't lacking motivation, they were lacking a moment of stability to work from. That bet was right, and the evidence has compounded since you stepped back. Demand from people ready to do exactly what Marisol did has pushed the program into three new zip codes, and a waiting list of 47 people is moving into the new year.

That list is where the next chapter lives. Right now, $1,200 moves one person off it and into a twelve-week counseling track — the same track Marisol used to negotiate her way back to stable ground. A decision of $3,600 clears three spots before February. If the waiting list was the right thing to act on then, it still is. You can make that call again at [LINK].
drafts.generic:
When Maria came to our housing stability program two years ago, she was three weeks from eviction, working two jobs, and running out of options. With help from our team — rental assistance, a budget coach, and a landlord mediator — she kept her apartment and, eventually, got back on solid ground. She is one of hundreds of stories like hers.

You made stories like Maria's possible when you gave to this program. Your generosity helped us build something real, and we have not forgotten that.

Since you last gave, we have expanded to three new neighborhoods and now serve twice as many families. We hope you will consider coming back to help us finish the year strong. A gift of any size before December 31st will go directly to keeping families housed this winter.
drafts.preference:
When Maria came to our housing stability program three years ago, she was two months behind on rent and one missed paycheck away from losing her apartment. She didn't need a dramatic rescue — she needed a short-term bridge and a plan. Your support helped provide both. Today she manages her own finances and, quietly remarkable, volunteers to help other families navigate the same process she once found overwhelming.

You gave because you believed stable housing changes everything downstream — school attendance, health, employment, family cohesion. That belief was right, and it still is. Since you last contributed, we've expanded from serving 200 households annually to nearly 500, adding financial counseling and landlord mediation services that have cut eviction rates in our network by 40 percent.

We'd love to welcome you back before December 31st. A gift of any amount helps, and a gift of $150 or more sponsors one household through a full month of stabilization support. If you're ready to reconnect, you can give online at [link] or return the enclosed envelope. We're glad you were part of this, and we hope you'll be part of what comes next.

user_reason: C is the strongest by a significant margin. Marisol is the subject of her own story — she reached out, she showed up, she negotiated, she signed. The donor is framed as someone who made a smart strategic read, not a generous benefactor. The ask connects directly back to Marisol's specific track with a concrete number tied to a concrete outcome. The 'that bet was right' framing is exactly the collaborator-investor voice I want. The only minor quibble is 'our housing counseling line' in the opening, but it's a small thing. A is solid second — Maria has some agency, the numbers are paired with her story, and the framing is warm and professional. But 'Your support helped provide both' makes the nonprofit the actor in her story, and the ask at the end is a bit generic rather than tightly connected to what Maria specifically did. B is the weakest. 'With help from our team' immediately strips Maria of agency and makes the nonprofit the hero. 'Your generosity helped us build something real' is exactly the virtuous-benefactor framing I try to avoid. The ask is vague — 'any size' with no specific outcome — and 'finish the year strong' is the kind of corporate fundraising language that makes me cringe.

criteria_analysis.name: Participant Agency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is outstanding: Marisol reaches out, shows up, negotiates, works out a plan — she is the grammatical subject with active, intentional verbs throughout, and her agency persists into the ask ('the same track Marisol used to negotiate her way back'). The generic draft fails badly: Maria 'came to our program' and then everything is done for her ('With help from our team — rental assistance, a budget coach, and a landlord mediator — she kept her apartment'). The original preference draft is mixed — Maria 'didn't need a dramatic rescue' and 'volunteers to help other families,' which shows some agency, but 'Your support helped provide both' and the framing of services added by the organization undercut it.

criteria_analysis.name: Warm, Story-Led Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with a specific, concrete story with vivid details (eviction notice, every appointment, repayment plan on her own terms, two-year lease extension) and reads like a personal letter with conversational directness. The original preference draft also opens with a strong story and earns its emotion through detail ('one missed paycheck away'), though it slides slightly into a more report-like register in the second paragraph. The generic draft opens with a story but it's vague and conventional ('running out of options,' 'got back on solid ground'), and the overall tone reads more like a standard fundraising template.

criteria_analysis.name: Organization Decentered
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is excellent — program expansion is framed as demand-driven ('Demand from people ready to do exactly what Marisol did has pushed the program into three new zip codes'), and the organization barely appears as a subject. The generic draft centers the organization repeatedly ('we have expanded,' 'we hope you will consider,' 'we serve'), making it the hero. The original preference draft falls between: 'we've expanded from serving 200 households annually to nearly 500, adding financial counseling and landlord mediation services' puts the organization squarely as the agent of expansion, even though the participant story is strong.

criteria_analysis.name: Story-to-Ask Continuity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels: Marisol's name and specific track persist into the ask ('the same track Marisol used to negotiate her way back to stable ground'), the emotional thread is seamless, and it ends with a definitive, confident closing. The generic draft completely disconnects — Maria vanishes after paragraph one, the ask is generic ('a gift of any size'), and it ends with a vague seasonal appeal. The original preference draft maintains some continuity through the housing stability theme but Maria disappears from the closing paragraph, and 'We're glad you were part of this' is exactly the kind of trailing, generic sign-off this user flags.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Framing as Strategist
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails this: 'You saw something like this coming,' 'You read the situation correctly,' 'That bet was right,' 'A decision of $3,600' — all strategic, decision-oriented language with zero gratitude framing. The generic draft is pure gratitude: 'Your generosity helped us build something real, and we have not forgotten that.' The original preference draft is mixed — 'You gave because you believed stable housing changes everything downstream' shows some strategic recognition, but 'Your support helped provide both' and 'We'd love to welcome you back' lean toward traditional donor appreciation.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete, Grounded Ask
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft provides exact, visible math: $1,200 per person, $3,600 for three spots, 47-person waiting list, twelve-week track, February deadline — the reader can do the math themselves. The original preference draft includes $150 per household per month, a December 31st deadline, and a clear mechanism (online or envelope), which is solid though less precisely layered. The generic draft has no specific numbers at all — 'a gift of any size' with only 'December 31st' as a deadline.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion — it sustains participant agency through active verbs and grammatical subjects, frames the donor as a strategist who 'read the situation correctly,' decenters the organization by attributing expansion to community demand, maintains Marisol's name and story through the ask, and provides concrete math the reader can act on. The original preference draft is competent and warm but lets the organization creep in as the hero, loses Maria in the closing, and defaults to gratitude framing. The generic draft reads like a standard fundraising template that violates nearly every criterion this user cares about.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts are clear, but Draft B communicates its message more directly and simply. Draft A uses more layered, implicit reasoning (e.g., 'You saw something like this coming') that requires more reader inference.

Draft A has a tightly structured narrative arc — story, validation of the reader's past decision, specific ask — where each paragraph flows logically into the next. Draft B is coherent but its transitions are more conventional and less tightly linked.
Both drafts are grammatically clean with no errors or typos.

Draft A follows a precise three-paragraph structure (anecdote, reader validation with evidence, specific call to action) that builds momentum effectively. Draft B has sound organization but is more formulaic in its nonprofit appeal structure.

Draft A is markedly more engaging — Marisol's agency is vividly rendered, the specific details (47-person waiting list, $1,200 per person) create urgency, and the reader is positioned as a strategic decision-maker rather than a generic donor. Draft B relies on familiar nonprofit language ('stories like hers,' 'finish the year strong') that, while competent, doesn't stand out.

Draft A's voice is distinctive — confident, respectful, and treats the reader as an intelligent partner rather than a passive giver. Draft B's tone is warm and appropriate but more generic and conventional for a fundraising appeal.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall: it features a more distinctive voice, more compelling engagement through specific details and narrative agency, and a more sophisticated rhetorical structure that respects the reader's intelligence. Draft B is competent and clear but reads as a standard nonprofit appeal without the same level of craft or memorability.
